authorities.name: California Department of Fish and Wildlife
Eldorado National Forest, USDA Forest Service

sources.title: CCR T14 §5.85(a)(1) — Trout, General Statewide Regulations (inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

sources.title: CCR T14 §7.40 — Alphabetical List of Hatchery Trout, Hatchery Steelhead, and Salmon Waters with Special Fishing Regulations (McConnell Lake not listed)
CCR T14 §7.50 — Alphabetical List of Trout Waters with Special Fishing Regulations (McConnell Lake not listed)
2026 California Freshwater Sport Fishing Regulations (Updated 06/01/2026)
sources.quotedText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and 10 trout possession limit.

sources.title: California Fish and Game Code §7145 — Sport Fishing License Requirement
sources.quotedText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

sources.title: CDFW Memorandum (10/22/2015) — Sierra Nevada Yellow-Legged Frog Monitoring in the Phipps Creek Planning Watershed within Desolation Wilderness
sources.quotedText: McConnell Lake (Figure 2) and its tributaries contain a small population of golden trout. No records exist of CDFW fish plants in McConnell Lake; the fish likely became established in McConnell after being planted in the Leland Lakes. Larval SNYLF are regularly seen in McConnell Lake. McConnell Lake is shallow and characterized by emergent vegetation and complex meadow tributaries where golden trout of all sizes are easily observed. Mechanical fish removal is not feasible at McConnell Lake (CDFW 2012), but a fish passage barrier approximately 500 meters above McConnell Lake prevents trout from ascending back into the Leland Lakes.

sources.title: CDFW Memorandum (23 March 2021) — Native Amphibian Monitoring in Desolation Wilderness; Rana sierrae Monitoring at Lake Zitella
sources.quotedText: The Aquatic Biodiversity Management Plan (ABMP) for the Desolation Wilderness Management Unit identifies Lake Zitella, Horseshoe Lake, McConnell Lake, Leland Lakes, and 4-Q Lakes as a Native Species Reserve (NSR) for SNYLF (CDFG 2012).

sources.title: Desolation Wilderness Permit — Eldorado National Forest, Recreation.gov
sources.quotedText: Visitors must obtain a Wilderness permit for overnight camping as well as day visits, year-round. A quota system is in place for the 45 overnight destination zones from the Friday before Memorial Day through September 30th each year.

parameters.note: McConnell Lake is not listed in CCR §7.40(b) or §7.50(b), so it falls under the general statewide lake regulation in §5.85(a)(1).

regulations.verbatimText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

parameters.counting_rule: General statewide trout limit for inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds: five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

regulations.verbatimText: All inland lakes, reservoirs, and ponds, including those on private lands, are open to fishing all year with a five-trout daily bag limit and ten-trout possession limit.

parameters.note: Anglers 16 years of age or older must have a valid California sport fishing license.

regulations.verbatimText: Except as otherwise provided in this article, every person 16 years of age or older who takes any fish, reptile, or amphibian for any purpose other than profit shall first obtain a valid license for that purpose...

parameters.description: McConnell Lake lies within the Desolation Wilderness (Eldorado National Forest); a Wilderness permit is required year-round for both day visits and overnight camping, with a quota system for overnight destination zones from the Friday before Memorial Day through September 30.

raw.quota_period: Friday before Memorial Day through September 30

regulations.citation: Recreation.gov — Desolation Wilderness Permit (Eldorado National Forest)
regulations.humanSummary: A Desolation Wilderness permit is required year-round for both day-use and overnight access, with a quota system for overnight zones from late May through September 30
regulations.verbatimText: Visitors must obtain a Wilderness permit for overnight camping as well as day visits, year-round. A quota system is in place for the 45 overnight destination zones from the Friday before Memorial Day through September 30th each year.

parameters.description: McConnell Lake and its tributaries hold a naturalized (non-planted) population of golden trout that drifted down from historic plants in the Leland Lakes; CDFW has no record of ever directly stocking McConnell Lake. The lake and watershed are part of a CDFW/USFS Native Species Reserve for the Sierra Nevada yellow-legged frog (Rana sierrae), and mechanical fish removal there has been judged infeasible due to the lake's shallow, meadow/marsh habitat.

regulations.citation: CDFW Memorandum (10/22/2015) — Phipps Creek PWS SNYLF Monitoring
regulations.humanSummary: Golden trout at McConnell Lake are a naturalized, unplanted population (no CDFW stocking record); the lake sits inside a designated Native Species Reserve for the Sierra Nevada yellow-legged frog
regulations.verbatimText: McConnell Lake (Figure 2) and its tributaries contain a small population of golden trout. No records exist of CDFW fish plants in McConnell Lake; the fish likely became established in McConnell after being planted in the Leland Lakes. Larval SNYLF are regularly seen in McConnell Lake. McConnell Lake is shallow and characterized by emergent vegetation and complex meadow tributaries where golden trout of all sizes are easily observed. Mechanical fish removal is not feasible at McConnell Lake (CDFW 2012), but a fish passage barrier approximately 500 meters above McConnell Lake prevents trout from ascending back into the Leland Lakes.
